title: BEEF
content:
The Theology of Beef (Conflict & Mortality)
This report details the structural mechanics of conflict in hip-hop, analyzing major feuds, the transition from lyrical disputes to lethal violence, and the "scorekeeping" culture of modern gang warfare.

1. The Coastal Wars: The Template for Tragedy
The East Coast vs. West Coast feud (1994–1997) established the paradigm for hip-hop conflict: a mixture of personal slight, corporate rivalry, and gang affiliation.

The Catalyst: The feud began as a friendship between Tupac Shakur and The Notorious B.I.G. but disintegrated after Shakur was shot at Quad Studios in NYC in 1994. He attributed the setup to Biggie’s circle.   

Corporate Warfare: Suge Knight (Death Row Records) weaponized the personal rift to disrupt Bad Boy Records' market dominance. Knight posted Shakur's $1.4 million bail in exchange for signing him, effectively drafting a soldier for his corporate war.   

Lethal Outcome: The conflict resulted in the unsolved murders of Shakur (Las Vegas, 1996) and Biggie (Los Angeles, 1997). These deaths proved that commercial success offered no immunity against street politics.   

2. The Drill Era: High-Velocity Retaliation
Modern "Drill" music, specifically from Chicago, shifted beef from commercial rivalry to "scorekeeping" of actual murders.

King Von vs. FBG Duck: This conflict represented the proxy war between the Black Disciples (O-Block) and Gangster Disciples (Tookaville). Unlike 90s beefs, which were often fought via subliminal lyrics, Drill beefs involve direct "bounties" and mocking the dead.   

The Hit: FBG Duck was assassinated in Chicago's Gold Coast in broad daylight in August 2020. King Von was killed months later in Atlanta. In 2024, six members of the O-Block faction were convicted for Duck's murder, which was revealed to be a contract killing to maintain gang status.   

3. The Memphis "Crab Bucket": Young Dolph vs. Yo Gotti
This feud illustrates the dangers of independent success in a territory dominated by established "bosses."

The Rejection: The conflict began when Young Dolph rejected a record deal from Yo Gotti’s CMG label to remain independent.   

Escalation: In 2017, Dolph's SUV was shot over 100 times in Charlotte; he survived due to custom bulletproof plating.

The Assassination: In November 2021, Dolph was murdered at Makeda’s Cookies in Memphis. Prosecutors alleged the hit was authorized by figures connected to Gotti's brother, Big Jook (who was subsequently murdered in 2024), highlighting the generational cycle of retaliation.   

4. Roll Call of the Fallen (Notable Murders)
Scott La Rock (1987): Early Bronx pioneer, killed attempting to mediate a dispute.   

Big L (1999): Harlem lyricist, killed in a drive-by attributed to a botched hit on his brother.   

Jam Master Jay (2002): Run-DMC legend, killed in his studio over a dispute involving 10 kilos of cocaine. Convictions were finally secured in 2024.   

Pookie Loc (2005): Jeezy associate killed by Gucci Mane during a home invasion. Gucci Mane was acquitted on self-defense grounds, sparking a decades-long feud.   

Pop Smoke (2020): Killed during a home invasion in Hollywood Hills after accidentally posting his address on Instagram.   

Nipsey Hussle (2019): Killed by Eric Holder in front of his Marathon Clothing store following a dispute about "snitching."

title: EVENTS
content:
Events (Historical Timeline)
A chronological log of pivotal moments that defined the industry and the streets.

1973: DJ Kool Herc throws the "Back to School Jam" at 1520 Sedgwick Avenue, widely cited as the birth of hip-hop.   

1985: The Crack Explosion: Crack cocaine floods major US cities. "Reality Rap" emerges as artists like Schoolly D and Ice-T document the violence.   

1991: The Balcony Incident: Suge Knight allegedly dangles Vanilla Ice over a hotel balcony to force him to sign over royalties to "Ice Ice Baby." This capital helps fund Death Row Records.   

1995: The Source Awards: Suge Knight attacks P. Diddy publicly ("If you don't want your executive producer... all in the videos... come to Death Row"), formally igniting the East/West Coast war.   

1996 (Sept 7): Tupac Shakur is shot in Las Vegas; he dies six days later.

1997 (March 9): The Notorious B.I.G. is murdered in Los Angeles.

2000 (May 24): 50 Cent Shooting: 50 Cent is shot nine times in Queens, allegedly on orders from Kenneth "Supreme" McGriff for referencing the Supreme Team in the song "Ghetto Qur'an."   

2005: BMF Takedown: The DEA dismantles the Black Mafia Family, seizing $14 million and indicting the Flenory brothers.   

2006: Birdman/Wayne Kiss: A photo of Birdman kissing Lil Wayne on the lips surfaces, sparking years of debate regarding the insular "mob" dynamics of Cash Money Records.   

2015: Drake vs. Meek Mill: Meek Mill accuses Drake of using ghostwriter Quentin Miller. Drake wins the feud via "Back to Back," proving audiences care more about hit songs than authorship purity.   

2022: YSL RICO Indictment: Young Thug and Gunna are arrested. Prosecutors use rap lyrics as evidence of gang activity, marking a major legal shift in how art is prosecuted.   

2024: Diddy Raids: Federal agents raid Sean "Diddy" Combs' properties in LA and Miami amid sex trafficking investigations, leading to the disintegration of his business empire.

title: CULTURE
content:
The Culture (Economics & Sociology)
This report analyzes the socio-economic conditions that birthed hip-hop, the "Trap" economy, and the archetypes of the industry.

1. The Crucible of the South Bronx (1970s)
Hip-hop emerged as a survival mechanism in the South Bronx following the "benign neglect" of the 1970s.

Urban Decay: Deindustrialization and the construction of the Cross Bronx Expressway destroyed the local economy. Landlords, unable to make profits, resorted to arson for insurance money, turning the borough into rubble.   

The Party as Resistance: Denied access to traditional music education, youth repurposed city infrastructure—streetlights for power, turntables for instruments—to create the "Block Party," the foundational social unit of the culture.   

2. The Crack Economy (1984–1990)
The introduction of crack cocaine fundamentally restructured the street hierarchy.

Supply-Side Innovation: A glut of powder cocaine in the early 80s caused wholesale prices to drop. Dealers converted powder to crack to sell smaller, cheaper units ($5–$20) to a mass market.   

Tournament Wages: Economist Sudhir Venkatesh found that street-level dealers earned roughly minimum wage ($3.30–$6.00/hour) while facing a 7% annual mortality risk. They accepted these odds for the "tournament" chance of becoming a boss.   

3. The Shadow Economy of Music
Laundering: Historically, drug kingpins (e.g., The Supreme Team, BMF) used record labels to launder illicit cash. Concert tours and merchandise sales, being cash-heavy, are ideal for mingling drug proceeds with legal revenue.   

Streaming Farms: In the modern era, "streaming farms" use bots to play songs on repeat. Criminals pay for these services with dirty money, and the resulting royalty checks from platforms like Spotify are "clean" taxable income.   

4. Archetypes: Boss vs. Wanksta
The Boss: A figure who holds capital and territory (e.g., Jay-Z, J. Prince). They often transition from illicit trade to corporate ownership.

The Wanksta: A term popularized by 50 Cent to describe a "fake gangster"—someone who projects the image of criminal wealth but lacks the history or the finances to back it up.

title: LEGENDS
content:
Legends (Mythology & Urban Legends)
A collection of the tall tales, verified myths, and "studio gangster" stories that color the culture.

1. The Illuminati & Blood Sacrifices
A persistent conspiracy theory posits that a secret society controls hip-hop, requiring "blood sacrifices" (the death of a loved one) for fame.

Origin: Rooted in 90s references to the "New World Order" (Prodigy of Mobb Deep).

The Myth: Deaths of figures like Tupac, Biggie, and Young Dolph are often reinterpreted by conspiracy theorists as ritual sacrifices rather than street violence.   

2. The Studio Gangsters
The "Studio Gangster" is an artist who fabricates a criminal past for marketability.

Rick Ross (Officer Ricky): In 2008, it was revealed that Rick Ross (William Roberts), who rapped about being a drug kingpin, had worked as a correctional officer. He survived the scandal by consistently releasing high-quality music, proving that "character" matters more than "truth" to modern fans.   

Slim Jesus: In 2015, a scrawny white teenager released "Drill Time," waving guns and rapping about murder. He later admitted he "didn't live that life." Unlike Ross, his lack of musical staying power turned him into a parody, and his career collapsed.   

3. The Unverified & The Bizarre
The KFC Bucket: An urban legend claims Ghostface Killah once buried a bucket of KFC in a producer's backyard to ensure no one else could eat it.   

Suge Knight's "Red" Urine: Rumors persisted that Suge Knight forced staff to drink his urine, or that he injected Eazy-E with AIDS—a theory Knight himself jokingly alluded to in a Jimmy Kimmel interview.   

4. Alpo & Rich Porter
The ultimate betrayal narrative. Alpo Martinez killed his partner and best friend Rich Porter in 1990 over a business dispute ($50,000). Alpo later snitched to avoid the death penalty. He was released into witness protection but was murdered in a drive-by in Harlem in 2021, proving the streets have a long memory.

title: STREET_CODE
content:
Street Codes (Laws & Language)
A guide to the unwritten rules, hierarchy, and lexicon of the streets.

1. The Code of Silence (Omertà)
Snitching: The cardinal sin. Cooperating with law enforcement destroys street credibility.

Tekashi 6ix9ine: The most high-profile violator of this code. He testified against the Nine Trey Gangsta Bloods to avoid a 47-year sentence. While he retained some fame as a "troll," he is permanently branded a "rat" and cannot move freely in street circles.   

2. Organizational Hierarchy (The Tournament Model)
Kingpin / Don: The top executive (e.g., Larry Hoover, Big Meech). Often insulated from handling drugs directly.

Lieutenant: Mid-level management. Oversees territory and collects dues.

Soldier / Shooter: Enforcers who handle violence and security.

Runner / Foot Soldier: The lowest level. sells drugs on corners. High risk, low pay (often below minimum wage).   

3. Comprehensive Slang Dictionary
A-C

Bag: Money; a specific amount of cash.

Bando: Abandoned house used for selling/using drugs (Trap house).

Big Face: $100 bill.

Bird / Chicken: A kilogram of cocaine (derived from its wrapped shape).

Blick / Blicky: Handgun.

Brick: A kilogram of drugs; or a very long time in prison.

Cap / No Cap: Lie / Truth.

Crash Out: To commit a reckless crime that results in death or life imprisonment.

D-G

Drill: To shoot/kill; also a subgenre of rap music.

Drop: The location of a person or a drug package.

Finessing: Tricking or outsmarting someone out of money.

Gas: High-potency marijuana.

Ghost: To disappear or leave.

Green Light: An order authorizing a hit (murder) on someone.

H-P

Hitter: An assassin or shooter.

Ice: Jewelry/Diamonds.

Lick: A target for robbery; "Hitting a lick" means successfully robbing someone.

O.G.: Original Gangster; an elder with status.

Opp: Opposition; enemy gang member.

Paper: Money.

Pie: A kilogram of cocaine.

Pushin' P: Keeping it real/Player (popularized by Gunna).

R-Z

Racks: Thousands of dollars (usually in stacks).

Rat: Informant/Snitch.

Slime: Friend/Associate (YSL terminology).

Spin the Block: To return to an enemy's territory to attack again.

Stain: A robbery target; or a blemish on one's reputation.

Stick: Rifle or large gun (AK-47/AR-15).

Trap: The place where drugs are sold; the lifestyle of dealing.

Washing: Money laundering.

Zip: An ounce of drugs.
